title: Device control derives exact authority from route coordinates
context: ADR-0005 established explicit Preview or Program production intents, expected-revision concurrency, operation-id idempotency, externally supplied direct-Program authority, and receipts that report server state rather than renderer observation. ADR-0009 established a device-only bearer authority bound to one Show, scopes, targets, controls, expiration, revocation, and generation, with fresh resolution for every action. ADR-0013 composed that lifecycle into the self-hosted server but intentionally left device request authentication and command execution unanswered. SDD-010 asked how one device HTTP request could cross into production visibility control without inheriting a Studio session or accepting authority from client-controlled payload data. Slice 1 mounted a dedicated device route outside interactive-session authentication, accepted exactly one Bearer credential from Authorization, rejected query, body, cookie, and duplicate credential transport, derived Show, target, and the canonical `<componentId>.visibility` control from route coordinates, and freshly called DeviceCredentialLifecycle.authorize for component.visibility:write before checking active Show state or invoking ProductionService. Tests demonstrated Studio-principal separation, exact Show, scope, target, and control denial, rotation, revocation, expiration, tampering, Preview isolation, explicit Program capability, closed body shape, revision conflicts, idempotent replay, operation conflicts, missing components, non-cacheable bounded errors and receipts, and a successful local in-process HTTP response within three seconds. Full governance, server, repository, build, and audit evidence passed. The human authority accepted H-010 only within that HTTP visibility-intent boundary.
decision: The self-hosted device Control API is a principal boundary separate from Studio authentication. A device visibility request is accepted only at `POST /api/device/shows/:showId/production/:target/components/:componentId/visibility` with exactly one syntactically valid Bearer credential in the Authorization header and no Cookie header or query parameters. The JSON body is closed to `visible`, `operationId`, and `expectedRevision`; it cannot name or override a Show, scope, target, component, control, role, or direct-Program capability. For every request, the server derives the exact Show and Preview or Program target from the route, derives the control identifier as `<componentId>.visibility`, fixes the scope to `component.visibility:write`, and freshly delegates authorization to the protocol lifecycle. Invalid or inactive bearers fail with a bounded 401 challenge, while a valid bearer lacking exact authority fails with a bounded 403 challenge. Only after exact authorization may the server confirm that the Show exists and is active and pass the route-derived intent to ProductionService. Program authorization is supplied to ProductionService only because the freshly authorized route target is Program; it is never accepted from the body. Successful and failed device responses are non-cacheable. The returned receipt describes resulting server state and preserves expected-revision and operation-id semantics; it does not prove network delivery, renderer application, OBS state, or device feedback.
consequences: A device bearer resolves only device authority and never creates, authenticates, or reuses a Studio principal or session.
Device clients must send one Authorization bearer and must not use cookies, query parameters, or JSON fields as credential transport.
Owners grant a visibility action by issuing the canonical `<componentId>.visibility` control identifier together with the exact Show, scope, and allowed Preview or Program targets.
The request body expresses desired visibility and concurrency identity but cannot grant production authority.
Rotation, revocation, expiration, and verifier checks affect the next action because authorization resolves the credential afresh for every request.
A credential authorized for Preview cannot mutate Program, and direct Program mutation remains an external capability proven before ProductionService executes the intent.
Authorization precedes Show lookup, which avoids disclosing Show state to an invalid or insufficient bearer; active-Show status still is not linearizable with a concurrent archival operation.
The demonstrated three-second budget covers only a local in-process HTTP test ending in a server-state receipt, not network, renderer, OBS, or hardware confirmation.

Action discovery, credential listing and response-loss recovery, cues, Take, value mutation, feedback transport, Companion, hardware support, per-device rate policy, audit retention, renderer observation, multi-process behavior, and in-flight authorization or archival linearizability remain separate questions.

policies.statement: A device Control API request must resolve only device authority and must never authenticate, construct, or inherit a Studio session principal.
Device control accepts exactly one Bearer credential from Authorization and must reject credential transport through query parameters, request bodies, cookies, or duplicate Authorization fields.

The device visibility route must derive Show, Preview or Program target, and canonical `<componentId>.visibility` control authority from route coordinates and must fix the required scope to component.visibility:write.

Every device action must resolve and authorize the bearer against the exact route-derived authority before Show lookup or production mutation so rotation, expiration, revocation, and scope changes fail closed on the next request.
A device visibility body may contain only visible, operationId, and expectedRevision and must not name or override Show, scope, target, component, control, role, or direct-Program authority.
ProductionService may receive direct Program authorization from the device route only after the same request bearer has been freshly authorized for the exact Program target and control.
Device visibility execution must preserve ProductionService expected-revision, operation-id replay, operation-id conflict, Preview isolation, and bounded server-state receipt semantics.

Passing local device HTTP tests may not be presented as proof of network delivery, renderer or OBS application, device feedback, catalogs, cues, Take, value mutation, Companion or hardware support, device-specific rate limits, audit retention, multi-process coordination, or in-flight revocation or Show-archival linearizability.
